instructions.evidence: Specific citations 6/10 — Has a date (19 March 2026), price (AED 1,200), named bodies (DHA, MOHAP), but few specific names, clinics, or case details.
Intro earns attention 4/10 — The strong hook exists but is buried under TL;DR, bylines, and repeated boilerplate before the reader gets to it.

patch.target: TL;DR: A Q1 health check is a 15-minute quarterly review that realigns your wellness goals with your current life circumstances before seasonal changes disrupt your routine.
patch.before: TL;DR: A Q1 health check is a 15-minute quarterly review that realigns your wellness goals with your current life circumstances before seasonal changes disrupt your routine.
patch.after: By March, half my Dubai clients have binned their January goals. Here's the 15-minute audit I run before the heat kills the rest.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Intro earns attention" scored 4/10: The strong hook exists but is buried under TL;DR, bylines, and repeated boilerplate before the reader gets to it.

instructions.evidence: specificity 6/10 — The post has strong specificity in places (AED 1,200, March 19 date, named tests like fasting glucose and lipid panel, DHA/MOHAP named bodies). But the practical adaptation section drifts into abstraction: 'hotter months,' 'local vegetables,' 'midday jolt drinks,' 'energy challenges,' and 'current conditions' are vague placeholders. The nutrition section lists 'whole grains, legumes, and local vegetables' without naming a single item (lentils, lupin, Arabic cucumber). The behaviour-change advice uses generic wellness language — 'positive routines,' 'long-term health change' — without a concrete before/after scenario anchored in Dubai life.

patch.target: Shifting a gym target to a brisk evening walk during hotter months is a sign of self-awareness, not failure.
patch.before: Shifting a gym target to a brisk evening walk during hotter months is a sign of self-awareness, not failure.
patch.after: Swapping a 6am gym session for a 7pm walk along JBR or around Al Safa Park — where temperatures drop to roughly 28°C by late April — is a sign of self-awareness, not failure.
patch.rationale: Quality axis "specificity" scored 6/10: The post has strong specificity in places (AED 1,200, March 19 date, named tests like fasting glucose and lipid panel, DHA/MOHAP named bodies). But the practical adaptation section drifts into abstraction: 'hotter months,' 'local vegetables,' 'midday jolt drinks,' 'energy challenges,' and 'current conditions' are vague placeholders. The nutrition section lists 'whole grains, legumes, and local vegetables' without naming a single item (lentils, lupin, Arabic cucumber). The behaviour-change advice uses generic wellness language — 'positive routines,' 'long-term health change' — without a concrete before/after scenario anchored in Dubai life.

Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated

Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
Specific human voice vs committee 4/10 — Mostly corporate editorial voice with occasional injected 'spicy' lines that feel pasted in.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 5/10 — A few pointed opinions surface (lab numbers, annual physicals are theatre) but most claims are hedged.
Unexpected phrasings 6/10 — Some vivid lines ('quietly rotting through spring') but diluted by generic SEO boilerplate.
Point of view vs survey 5/10 — Takes a mild stance but mostly surveys recommendations neutrally.
Quotable sentence 6/10 — 'Annual physicals are theatre' and 'the desert humbles every calendar' are quotable; rest is forgettable.
Intro earns attention 4/10 — The strong hook exists but is buried under TL;DR, bylines, and repeated boilerplate before the reader gets to it.

intro_hook 4/10 — The opening sentence is a generic demographic statement with zero tension or specificity. It's functionally identical to 'In today's world, many people...' The TL;DR above it is strong, but the actual prose hook is flat and earns no attention. The body later contains a genuinely compelling line ('By mid-March, I've watched half my clients quietly abandon January's goals') buried in Key Takeaways—that energy needs to open the post.

specificity 6/10 — The post has strong specificity in places (AED 1,200, March 19 date, named tests like fasting glucose and lipid panel, DHA/MOHAP named bodies). But the practical adaptation section drifts into abstraction: 'hotter months,' 'local vegetables,' 'midday jolt drinks,' 'energy challenges,' and 'current conditions' are vague placeholders. The nutrition section lists 'whole grains, legumes, and local vegetables' without naming a single item (lentils, lupin, Arabic cucumber). The behaviour-change advice uses generic wellness language — 'positive routines,' 'long-term health change' — without a concrete before/after scenario anchored in Dubai life.
